<!-- Topic 1: Binary Search -->
<!-- Total slides: 6 -->

# Binary Search

---

## Can we search faster than scanning every element? {.smaller}

- Linear search checks every element — worst case is O(N)
- Binary search cuts the search space in half at each step — but only works on sorted data

::: notes
Total slides: 6
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## The algorithm

```
sorted: { 62, 78, 84, 91, 95 }
          low           high
```

1. Set `low = 0`, `high = SIZE - 1`
2. Compute `mid = (low + high) / 2`
3. If `arr[mid]` equals the target — found
4. If target is less than `arr[mid]` — search the left half (`high = mid - 1`)
5. If target is greater than `arr[mid]` — search the right half (`low = mid + 1`)
6. Repeat until found or `low > high`

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Implementation

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int binarySearch(int arr[], int size, int target);

int main() {
    int scores[5] = {62, 78, 84, 91, 95};
    int result = binarySearch(scores, 5, 84);
    if (result != -1)
        cout << "Found at index " << result << "\n";
    else
        cout << "Not found\n";
    return 0;
}

int binarySearch(int arr[], int size, int target) {
    int low  = 0;
    int high = size - 1;
    while (low <= high) {
        int mid = (low + high) / 2;
        if (arr[mid] == target) return mid;
        if (target < arr[mid])  high = mid - 1;
        else                    low  = mid + 1;
    }
    return -1;  // not found
}
```

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Trace — searching for 84

```
array:  { 62, 78, 84, 91, 95 }   indices 0–4

Step 1: low=0  high=4  mid=2  arr[2]=84  → found at index 2
```

Searching for 91:

```
Step 1: low=0  high=4  mid=2  arr[2]=84  84 < 91  → low = 3
Step 2: low=3  high=4  mid=3  arr[3]=91  → found at index 3
```

- Two comparisons to find 91 in five elements — linear search averages 2.5

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Performance

| Search | Worst case | Requires sorted data? |
|---|---|---|
| Linear | O(N) | No |
| Binary | O(log₂ N) | Yes |

- For 1,000 elements: linear checks up to 1,000; binary checks at most 10
- The trade-off: the array must be sorted before every search — sorting costs O(N log N)
- If you search rarely, sort once and search many times; otherwise linear search may be simpler

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## Summary

- Binary search halves the search space at each step, reaching O(log N) performance
- It requires a sorted array — unsorted data produces incorrect results

<!-- Slide 6 -->
